planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic, lean to average soil in full sun; Florida mountain mint grows naturally in moist pine flatwoods, flatwood pond margins, and open, moist sandy areas in Florida and adjacent Gulf Coast states.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic, lean to average soil in full sun; Florida mountain mint is an endemic species of Florida and the Gulf Coastal Plain, adapted to the subtropical growing conditions of zone 8–10 where more northern mountain mints fail in summer heat and humidity. Like all Pycnanthemum species, it produces small white flowers with purple speckling in dense, flat-topped clusters in summer — an absolute magnet for native bees, especially small sweat bees and Halictid bees that are specialist mountain mint pollinators. The crushed foliage is intensely minty-aromatic, similar to wild mint but more pungent. Mountain mints are among the top-ranked native plants for native bee diversity in scientific studies. This Gulf-adapted species fills the role of mountain mint in zones 8–10 where slender mountain mint (P. tenuifolium) is the most common alternative.

planting.note: Plant container-grown cuttings in spring in very well-drained, dry, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy wicky is a specialist of dry, sandy, acidic soils in the longleaf pine ecosystem and will not tolerate moist or fertile conditions.

directions: Plant in dry, lean, sandy, strongly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy wicky is a diminutive evergreen shrub of the longleaf pine ecosystem of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, growing in the driest, most nutrient-poor, acidic white sands of pine scrub, sandhills, and dry pine flatwoods. Its characteristic small, hairy, needle-like leaves and low, spreading habit distinguish it from the large, showy mountain laurel (K. latifolia) of Appalachian forests. Despite its small stature, the flowers are disproportionately showy for the plant's size — the same distinctive cupped, saucer-like Kalmia blooms in bright pink with ten anthers tucked in pockets around the rim — produced in summer when few other shrubs bloom. An outstanding native for low-maintenance landscaping in dry, acidic sandy soils in zone 7–9 where mountain laurel would fail in summer heat.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; dense St. John's wort grows naturally in moist to wet soils at stream banks, pond margins, and in seasonally wet meadows. It tolerates periodic flooding better than most Hypericum species.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; dense St. John's wort is a native shrubby Hypericum of moist streambanks, pond margins, and wet woodland edges across the eastern United States. It produces abundant clusters of small, bright yellow flowers in summer that attract specialized pollen-collecting bees. Unlike the herbaceous St. John's worts used medicinally, this is a woody, multi-stemmed shrub that can reach 6 feet, providing structure in the wet garden. The tiny, linear leaves are produced in dense, crowded clusters along the stems — giving the species its name. It tolerates seasonal flooding and grows naturally in the same wet habitats as cardinal flower, swamp milkweed, and ironweed. An excellent shrub for rain gardens, bioswales, and wet meadow edges where it provides summer blooms and serves as a nectar source for native bees.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Maryland dittany grows naturally in dry, rocky, open woodlands and woodland edges across the eastern United States, preferring thin, well-drained, slightly acidic soils.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Maryland dittany — also called stone mint or wild oregano — is a low-growing native perennial herb of dry, rocky woodland edges and open, thin-soiled habitats across the eastern United States. It produces small lavender-pink flowers in late summer when most other native herbs have finished blooming, making it valuable for late-season nectar. The entire plant is strongly aromatic with a fresh, oregano-like fragrance when bruised, reflecting its culinary history as a wild herb — the dried leaves can be used as a substitute for oregano or dittany in cooking and teas. One of the few native mints that blooms in late summer and fall, providing critical resources for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season native bees. Extremely drought tolerant once established in appropriate dry, rocky conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, average to slightly lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; hoary mountain mint is tolerant of a wider range of soil conditions than many mountain mints and grows naturally in open woodlands, woodland edges, rocky slopes, and dry meadows.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; hoary mountain mint is one of the most distinctive mountain mints, immediately recognizable by the striking silvery-white, downy (hoary) coating on the upper leaves and bracts surrounding the flower clusters — creating a bold, almost mealy-white appearance in midsummer that makes the plant ornamentally attractive even before and after bloom. The white bracts below the small lavender flowers are so conspicuous that they resemble petals, creating an unusual effect in the garden. Like all mountain mints, the flowers attract an extraordinary diversity of native bees — documented as supporting over 50 native bee species at a single planting. The aromatic foliage is repellent to deer. More tolerant of dry, rocky soils and partial woodland shade than Virginia mountain mint, hoary mountain mint is excellent in the transitional zone between sunny meadow and shaded woodland.
